title: Incheon crowd forecast and safety-threshold dossier
headline_assessment: Crowd-risk failure can happen when expected attendance is forecast below a formal planning threshold, or when traffic/egress demand is underestimated even if the event itself is attractive.
confidence: B+ for risk mechanism using public reports and official safety-policy sources; case-specific official after-action records still missing.
core_lesson: A festival does not need to be a total failure to reduce satisfaction. A short successful show can still fail operationally if exit traffic, bottlenecks, communication and safety staffing are underplanned.

policy_context.finding: Safety management planning applies to festivals with momentary maximum attendance of 1,000 or more, or festivals held on mountains/water, or using fire, fireworks or flammable gas.
Main inspection items include crowd bottlenecks, safety staffing, weather, traffic control, facilities, fire, rescue/medical response, high-risk programs and festival insurance.
Incheon describes a four-stage crowd-safety system: risk status investigation, planning and capacity/safety staffing, police/fire/expert pre-check, CCTV monitoring and emergency communication.

cases.observed_signal: Reported actual crowd reached about 1,000 at peak and 1,500 cumulative, while organizers reportedly expected under 1,000 and did not submit a safety management plan.
cases.why_it_matters: The venue was on a mountain with narrow access; celebrity/fan demand changed the risk beyond the initial estimate. The threshold itself became a blind spot.
cases.satisfaction_or_safety_effect: 112 report, police/fire/local officials deployed late, performance reportedly stopped for about 15 minutes, safety alert text sent.

cases.observed_signal: Reported spectators exceeded forecast by more than tenfold in one report, with severe road congestion and resident complaints after a short fireworks/drone show.
cases.why_it_matters: Fireworks are a high-risk program and island/bridge access creates predictable egress concentration. Traffic and communication planning are part of festival satisfaction.
cases.satisfaction_or_safety_effect: Reports described severe congestion, late traffic alert, insufficient detour guidance and lack of field guidance personnel.

why_chain.label: Demand signal
why_chain.finding: Performer popularity, fireworks/drone content, social spread and weather can change real demand quickly.

why_chain.label: Planning threshold
why_chain.finding: If expected attendance is set below the formal safety-plan trigger, staffing and route planning can be underbuilt.

why_chain.label: Site geometry
why_chain.finding: Mountain paths, bridges, island roads, narrow exits and informal standing zones can dominate risk more than raw attendance.

why_chain.label: Operational lag
why_chain.finding: Late deployment, late text alerts or post-event traffic guidance have low effect once crowd/vehicle flows have already formed.

why_chain.label: Satisfaction impact
why_chain.finding: A visually successful event can still produce dissatisfaction when egress, communication or safety staffing fails.

documents_to_collect_next: Expected-attendance calculation basis for each event
Safety management plan or official non-submission reason
Venue map with entrance/exit widths and bottleneck points
Traffic-control plan, parking plan, detour plan and public-transport plan
Timing of safety texts, road controls, staff deployment and event stoppage
After-action review, complaints and corrective measures
